exemplar.title: Investigating the recency effect in Multi-store Memory model

detailedExemplarData.title: Investigating the recency effect in Multi-store Memory model

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Aim is clearly stated and its relevance to realâworld applications is well explained
Multiâstore memory model is accurately described with clear links to the recency effect and delay manipulation
Hypotheses are directional and null, with IV and DV explicitly stated and operationalized
weaknesses: Description of sensory memory is ambiguous and could specify typical duration
Use of the term âoverwrittenâ lacks precision compared to standard interference terminology
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The introduction demonstrates a thorough understanding of the investigationâs aim, relevance, theoretical basis and operationalization of variables, fitting solidly in the 5â6 band. Minor imprecision in terminology and brevity in one aspect prevent a topâband mark, so a score of 5 is justified.

strengths: Independent-measures design is not only described but clearly justified against alternatives
Opportunity sampling and participant characteristics are described and their limitations explained
Materials and controlled variables are explained with rationale for each choice
weaknesses: Randomization procedure for group assignment is not specified
Procedural detail about practice trials and exact timing calibration of the rebus puzzle is missing
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Exploration is comprehensive and wellâjustified, satisfying all criteria for the top band (3â4). Only minor procedural clarifications are lacking, so the full mark of 4 is appropriate.

strengths: Descriptive (mean, SD) and inferential (MannâWhitney U) statistics are used appropriately
Findings are interpreted accurately and linked back to the hypothesis and model
weaknesses: Confusion between MannâWhitney U and t-test procedures and inconsistent reporting of test values
Graphical presentation omits error bars, axis labels and legend
Inconsistent rounding in statistical tables and unclear pâvalue specification
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While both descriptive and inferential statistics are applied and the interpretation is solid, persistent errors in statistical reporting and graph presentation place this work at the lower end of the 3â4 band. A score of 3 reflects these shortcomings.

strengths: Findings are discussed in relation to the multiâstore model and prior literature
Strengths and limitations are explained and clearly linked to aspects of the investigationâs validity
Modifications are explicitly tied to identified limitations and justified for improving the study
weaknesses: Discussion is slightly repetitive and could deepen analysis of distractor complexity
One sentence in the limitations section is incomplete, missing the full argument
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation meets most descriptors for the highest band, offering coherent discussion and justified modifications. Minor repetitiveness and a small lapse in completeness keep it from the very top, warranting a score of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar graph correctly depicts group means and addresses the hypothesis, but it lacks error bars and axis titles are minimal. These **presentation errors** align with the 3-4 descriptor (âaddresses the hypothesis but contains errorsâ). A mid-band score is appropriate.

The student states the statistical outcome, interprets it with respect to the data (no significant difference, cannot reject null) and explicitly links this to the hypothesis and the multi-store model. The logical flow is accurate; a minor overstatement (âpartial validationâ) prevents the very top mark, but the work fits solidly in the highest band.

Both IV (presence vs absence of a 30-s distractor) and DV (number of last five words recalled) are **explicitly stated and operationalised**. The hypotheses are correctly formatted as directional and null versions. No ambiguity remains, matching the highest descriptor; full band score awarded.

Opportunity sampling is described and its limitations for generalisability are **explained**; demographic characteristics are detailed. The rationale for not using a larger or random sample is given. This meets the explanatory requirement, earning the top of the 3-4 band.

The multi-store memory model is **accurately described** (sensory, STM, LTM; rehearsal and encoding) and the student explicitly connects the recency effect and delay manipulation to the model. The explanation of how rehearsal displacement underlies the predicted effect shows a clear link. Occasional wording inaccuracies (e.g., âoverwrittenâ) are negligible, so the work fits comfortably in the 5-6 band, mid-band score given.

Findings are not merely described; they are **discussed** in relation to the multi-store model and contrasted with Glanzer & Cunitz. The student explores why the delay might not fully eliminate the recency effect. Discussion depth is good but not exceptionally sophisticated, so a score of 5 within the top band is merited.

The student clearly states the aim (to test the effect of a 30-s delay on the recency effect) and **explains its relevance** by linking it to real-world applications (teaching, advertising) and to understanding STM/LTM interaction. This goes beyond mere identification, fulfilling the descriptor for the 5-6 band. Minor brevity in the real-world explanation prevents awarding the very top of the band.

Multiple strengths and limitations are **explained** and clearly linked to internal validity, sampling and task difficulty. Each point is relevant to the investigation, satisfying the 5-6 descriptor; slight repetitiveness keeps it at 5 rather than 6.

Materials (word list, rebus puzzle, environment) and controlled variables (presentation duration, classroom setting) are presented with explicit **justification** for each choice (e.g., monosyllabic to reduce complexity). That moves beyond description into explanation, so the strand sits at the upper end of the 3-4 band.

Proposed modifications (larger sample, harder distractor, varied delays) are **explicitly tied to stated limitations** and justified in terms of how they would improve validity. This meets the highest descriptor; breadth is solid though depth could be richer, so a score of 5 is appropriate.

The student not only describes the independent-measures design but **explains** why it was chosen and contrasts it with alternatives, addressing order effects and participant variability. This satisfies the âexplainedâ criterion, so the 3-4 band applies; thorough justification merits the top of that band.

Descriptive (mean, SD) and inferential (Mann-Whitney U) statistics are appropriate to the design. However, the narrative confuses U with t-tests and reports a p-value without exact tails; the appendix shows some inconsistent U values. These minor **errors in application/reporting** place the work in the 3-4 band; lower score within the band awarded.

annotations.comment: The student provides a clear and concise definition of memory and introduces the multi-store memory model effectively, setting a solid theoretical foundation for the investigation.
Describing sensory memory as ânot controlled consciouslyâ is ambiguous; elaborate on its typical duration and how it contributes to encoding within the multi-store framework.
Ensure footnote citations follow APA format consistently and consolidate access dates at the end of the reference list for clarity.
The aim to replicate Glanzer & Cunitzâs delay effect is clearly stated and linked to educational and marketing applications, demonstrating strong relevance.

The term âoverwrittenâ to describe STM displacement lacks precision; consider replacing it with âdisplaced due to proactive or retroactive interferenceâ to align with standard memory terminology.
The research question explicitly frames the IV (30-s delay) and DV (recall of last five words), enabling clear operational linkage to the hypothesis.
Include an anticipated effect size or magnitude prediction in the directional hypothesis to support power considerations and clarify expectations.
The independent-measures design is well justified by addressing order effects and group matching, demonstrating thoughtful control of potential confounds.
Specify the randomization procedure used to assign participants to groups to enhance replicability and strengthen internal validity.
Opportunity sampling is well described and its generalisability limitations are discussed, showing critical awareness of sampling bias.
Consider incorporating stratified sampling or demographic quotas in future studies to mitigate opportunity sampling bias and improve representativeness.
The choice of monosyllabic words is well justified to minimise linguistic complexity and ensure consistency in recall difficulty.
Pretest the rebus puzzleâs cognitive load and consider more demanding distractors to ensure effective interference with rehearsal.
Ethical safeguards including informed consent, confidentiality, and right to withdraw are clearly addressed, demonstrating strong consideration of participant welfare.
Clarify whether participants received practice trials or timing cues to ensure uniform task performance across both conditions.
Specify how the rebus puzzleâs difficulty was calibrated to occupy exactly 30 seconds, improving procedural rigour.
Descriptive statistics (mean and SD) are presented clearly for both groups, facilitating straightforward comparison.
Include confidence intervals or standard error bars in the descriptive table to provide a fuller understanding of variability.
Explicitly connect the descriptive trend to the hypothesis by noting that the higher mean in the control group aligns with the prediction despite non-significance.
The description conflates MannâWhitney U and t-test procedures, indicating a misunderstanding of inferential methods; clarify which test is used.
Refine the inferential statistics table by labeling test statistics (U, Z) clearly and indicating whether the p-value is one- or two-tailed.
Correctly interprets pâ>â.05 and links it to the hypothesis decision, demonstrating solid understanding of non-significant findings.
The bar graph omits error bars, axis labels, and a legend, reducing clarity; add these elements for a more informative presentation.
The discussion effectively compares results to the multi-store model and prior studies, deepening the evaluation of findings.
Expand on how the complexity of different distractor tasks specifically accounts for the partial recency effect observed.
Strengths are clearly linked to internal validity and design choices, demonstrating critical reflection on the studyâs robustness.
Complete the final sentence on delay duration to fully articulate its impact as a limitation, ensuring the section reads as a cohesive argument.
Future directions are grounded in identified limitations, demonstrating coherent and well-justified evaluation of study improvements.
In the conclusion, restate the operational definitions of the IV and DV briefly to reinforce clarity on the study parameters.
Inconsistent rounding of the standard deviation values reduces precision; adopt a consistent two-decimal rounding convention in calculation appendices.
